title: Stocking-stitch dishcloth — beginner
subtitle: The smallest finished object that teaches the whole knitting loop
excerpt: A 22 × 22 cm cotton dishcloth in stocking stitch with a four-stitch garter border. The smallest meaningful finished object: it teaches casting on, knit, purl, stitch counting, gauge swatching, casting off, and weaving in ends — every move that scales up to a scarf or a jumper. Three evenings of knitting if you start fresh; an hour if the rhythm is already there.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Knitting (Victorian, public domain) — the cotton dishcloth and washcloth appear across multiple Weldon's volumes as the standard household-knit beginner project. Mary Thomas, Knitting Book (1938, out of copyright UK/EU) — describes stocking stitch (US: stockinette) as the first stitch combination every learner should master, after a few rows of plain garter. The four-stitch garter border to prevent stocking-stitch curl is a near-universal home-textile convention.

content.text: A cotton dishcloth is the smallest finished object that teaches the whole knitting loop. By the time the cloth comes off the needles, the knitter has cast on, knit a row, purled a row, counted a few hundred stitches, fixed at least one dropped stitch, knitted some rows of border, cast off, and woven in two ends. Every move scales up: the scarf is a longer dishcloth; the jumper is wider, longer, and shaped. The dishcloth is the proof-of-concept that the technique is in the hands.
This pattern is twenty-two stitches wide and sixty-six rows tall — about a hundred grams of DK cotton, less if the cloth is knit at a tighter tension. The body of the cloth is
(right-side knit rows, wrong-side purl rows) with a four-stitch
border on all four sides — the border stops the cloth from
. Three evenings of work if the cast-on, knit, and purl are still being learned; an hour if the rhythm is already there.

Mercerised cotton (DROPS Paris, Scheepjes Cahlista, Yarn and Colors Must-Have, Drops Muskat) holds gauge cleanly and washes well. Cotton is the right fibre for a dishcloth — wool felts in hot water, acrylic doesn't absorb. Any DK cotton in any colour works; pale neutrals show stains less than bright colours in a working dishcloth.
A pair of 4 mm straight needles.

The UK standard pairing for DK yarn. Bamboo or wood needles are easier for learners than slick metal — the stitches don't slide off as quickly. The needles need to be at least 25 cm long; 30–35 cm is more comfortable.
Tapestry needle, sharp scissors, and a soft measuring tape.
The tapestry needle is for weaving in the two yarn tails at the end; the measuring tape is for the gauge swatch and the finished cloth.
22 sts × 30 rows = 10 × 10 cm in stocking stitch on 4 mm needles in DK cotton, lightly steamed.

of about forty rows in stocking stitch before starting the cloth. Measure the central 10 × 10 cm — not the curling edges — and count the stitches across and the rows up. The dishcloth is forgiving of small gauge variance (it doesn't have to fit a body, only a draining board), but the swatch is the right place to learn how to measure gauge. Practising it on a cloth that doesn't need to be perfect builds the habit for jumpers and socks that do.

If you get 24 sts × 32 rows on the swatch (tight tension), move up to a 4.5 mm needle. If you get 20 sts × 28 rows (loose tension), drop to a 3.5 mm needle. The pattern below is written for the 22-stitch gauge — adjust the needle, not the cast-on count. A locking stitch marker clipped onto the first stitch of the cast-on row helps mark the right-side edge for the first few rows; once the stocking-stitch V-pattern is visible on the right side, the marker can come off.
Using the long-tail cast on, cast on 50 stitches. Measure a tail of about 1.5 m before the slip knot — 2.5 cm per stitch is 1.25 m, plus 15 cm spare for weaving in, plus a small margin for safety.
Row 1 (WS): Knit across all 50 sts. (Yes, knit the wrong side on a garter row.) Rows 2–6: Knit across all 50 sts every row. (50 sts; 6 ridges of garter on the right side.)
Body (rows 7–60, stocking stitch with garter borders).

Row 7 (RS): k50. Row 8 (WS): k4, p42, k4. (50 sts.) From here on, every odd row (RS) is k50; every even row (WS) is k4, p42, k4. The four knit stitches at each edge of the wrong-side rows form the garter side-borders that hold the stocking stitch flat. Repeat this two-row sequence until the body has 54 rows of stocking stitch (i.e. the cloth measures about 18 cm from the top of the garter border, by the gauge above).
Rows 61–66: Knit across all 50 sts every row. (50 sts; 6 ridges of garter on the right side, matching the bottom border.)
all 50 stitches knitwise. Cut the working yarn leaving a 15 cm tail.

Thread each tail onto the tapestry needle in turn. Weave the tail through five or six stitches along the back of the garter border, doubling back once for security. Trim close to the work. The garter border hides woven-in ends better than the stocking stitch body would; this is one reason home-textile patterns put the border there.

Lay the cloth flat on an ironing board. Hold a steam iron 2-3 cm above the cloth and pass it across the fabric — do not touch the iron to the cotton. Pat the cloth flat to the intended 22 × 22 cm dimensions while the steam relaxes the fibres. Cotton needs only a light pass; over-steamed cotton can flatten the texture out of the stocking stitch. A dishcloth doesn't strictly need blocking, but the first light steam evens out any tension drift across the rows and gives a clean finished edge.

A cotton dishcloth lives in the kitchen, gets hot soapy water through it daily, and lasts about a year of daily use before the fibres soften beyond useful. Machine wash on a 60°C cottons cycle alongside tea towels; tumble-dry low or air-dry. Cotton softens and slightly shrinks on the first hot wash — that's normal. The dishcloth will end up about 5% smaller than it came off the needles, which is built into the cast-on count above.

Knit every row all the way through (skip the stocking stitch section, just keep knitting). Garter stitch lies completely flat, doesn't curl, and uses about 15% more yarn than stocking stitch at the same dimensions because it's a denser fabric. A first dishcloth in garter is even simpler than this pattern; the trade-off is the loss of the smooth side.

Change colour every six or ten rows in the body section. Cut and re-join at each colour change, weaving in the ends along the garter border. Two colours of DK cotton give a useful kitchen cloth set that looks tidier than a single-colour set when hung.

For a denser, more scrubbing-suitable cloth, work the body in slip-stitch linen stitch (k1, slip 1 with yarn in front, repeat). The fabric is firm enough to scour a baking dish. Slip-stitch linen stitch is one rung up on the learning ladder; this dishcloth is the prerequisite.

gaugeText: 22 sts × 30 rows = 10 × 10 cm in stocking stitch on 4 mm needles in DK cotton, lightly steamed.

recipeTools.notes: For weaving in the cast-on and cast-off tails.
For checking the gauge swatch and the finished cloth.
Optional. A locking stitch marker on the right edge after the cast-on row helps track right-side vs wrong-side while learning.
